questions.question_text: What is the result of $1,000 invested at 5% annual interest compounded yearly for 1 year?
questions.explanation: A = 1000(1 + 0.05)^1 = 1000(1.05) = $1,050. The principal grows by 5% in one year.

questions.question_text: If $500 is invested at 10% annual interest compounded yearly, what is the balance after 2 years?
questions.explanation: A = 500(1.10)^2 = 500(1.21) = $605. After year 1 the balance is $550, and 10% of $550 is $55, giving $605.

questions.question_text: In the compound interest formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), what does 'n' represent?

options.text: The number of years
The number of compounding periods per year
The interest rate
The principal amount

questions.explanation: In the formula A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), 'n' represents the number of times interest is compounded per year (e.g., n = 12 for monthly).

questions.question_text: What is the value of (1 + 0.08/4) when interest is 8% compounded quarterly?
questions.explanation: Dividing 0.08 by 4 gives 0.02, so (1 + 0.02) = 1.02. Each quarter the balance grows by 2%.

questions.question_text: How much interest is earned on $2,000 at 6% compounded annually for 1 year?
questions.explanation: Interest = 2000 x 0.06 = $120. For one year compounded annually, compound and simple interest are the same.

questions.question_text: If interest is compounded monthly, how many compounding periods are there in 3 years?
questions.explanation: Monthly compounding means n = 12 periods per year. Over 3 years: nt = 12 x 3 = 36 compounding periods.

questions.question_text: What is the balance of $800 invested at 4% compounded annually for 3 years? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: A = 800(1.04)^3 = 800(1.124864) = $899.89. Each year the interest is calculated on the growing balance.

questions.question_text: Which compounding frequency produces the most interest over the same time period and rate?

options.text: Annually
Quarterly
Monthly
Daily

questions.explanation: The more frequently interest is compounded, the more total interest is earned because interest-on-interest accumulates faster. Daily compounding beats monthly, quarterly, and annual.

questions.question_text: What is the total amount after investing $1,200 at 3% compounded annually for 2 years?
questions.explanation: A = 1200(1.03)^2 = 1200(1.0609) = $1,273.08. The extra $1.08 beyond simple interest comes from compounding.

questions.question_text: If P = $400, r = 0.05, n = 2, and t = 1, what is A using A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt)?
questions.explanation: A = 400(1 + 0.05/2)^(2x1) = 400(1.025)^2 = 400(1.050625) = $420.25. Semi-annual compounding yields slightly more than annual.

questions.question_text: What is compound interest?

options.text: Interest calculated only on the original principal
Interest calculated on the principal plus previously earned interest
A flat fee charged for borrowing money
The total amount of money deposited over time

questions.explanation: Compound interest is interest calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods, causing the balance to grow exponentially.

questions.question_text: What does the variable 'P' represent in the compound interest formula?

options.text: The profit earned
The percentage rate
The principal (initial amount invested or borrowed)
The number of payment periods

questions.explanation: In A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt), P stands for the principal, which is the original amount of money invested or borrowed before any interest is applied.

questions.question_text: What does 'compounding period' mean?

options.text: The total length of the investment
The time interval at which interest is calculated and added to the balance
The number of years until the investment doubles
The day the investment matures

questions.explanation: A compounding period is the specific time interval (monthly, quarterly, annually, etc.) at which accumulated interest is calculated and added to the principal balance.

questions.question_text: What does 'annual percentage rate (APR)' mean in the context of compound interest?

options.text: The total interest earned over the life of the investment
The stated yearly interest rate before accounting for compounding
The monthly interest rate multiplied by 12
The effective rate after compounding is applied

questions.explanation: The APR is the nominal annual interest rate stated by the lender or bank. It does not reflect the effect of compounding; the actual yield may be higher depending on how often interest compounds.

questions.question_text: What does it mean for interest to be 'compounded quarterly'?

options.text: Interest is added once a year
Interest is added every 4 years
Interest is calculated and added 4 times per year
Interest is added every quarter of a month

questions.explanation: Compounded quarterly means interest is calculated and added to the balance four times per year (every three months), so n = 4 in the compound interest formula.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between simple interest and compound interest?

options.text: Simple interest uses a higher rate than compound interest
Compound interest is always less than simple interest
Simple interest is calculated only on the principal, while compound interest is calculated on the principal plus accumulated interest
There is no difference; they are the same calculation

questions.explanation: Simple interest is computed only on the original principal (I = Prt), whereas compound interest is computed on the principal plus any previously earned interest, leading to exponential growth.

questions.question_text: What does 'accrued amount' mean in compound interest?

options.text: Only the interest portion of the balance
The total amount including principal and all earned interest
The amount of money withdrawn from the account
The fee charged by the bank for the account

questions.explanation: The accrued amount (A) is the total value of the investment or loan, including both the original principal and all interest that has accumulated over time.

questions.question_text: What does 'annual percentage yield (APY)' mean?

options.text: The nominal interest rate stated on the account
The effective annual rate of return that accounts for the effect of compounding
The monthly interest rate
The total interest paid over the life of the loan

questions.explanation: APY (annual percentage yield) is the effective annual rate that reflects the actual amount earned after compounding. It is always equal to or higher than the stated APR when compounding occurs more than once a year.

questions.question_text: What does it mean to say an investment 'compounds continuously'?

options.text: Interest is never added to the balance
Interest is compounded once per year
Interest is compounded an infinite number of times per year, modeled by A = Pe^(rt)
The interest rate changes every day

questions.explanation: Continuous compounding means the compounding frequency approaches infinity. The formula becomes A = Pe^(rt), where e is approximately 2.71828. This represents the theoretical maximum growth.

questions.question_text: What does the term 'maturity' mean in the context of a compound interest investment?

options.text: The date when the investment was first made
The interest rate applied to the investment
The date when additional deposits are made
The date when the investment term ends and the full balance can be withdrawn

questions.explanation: Maturity refers to the end of the investment period, at which point the investor can withdraw the full accrued amount (principal plus all compounded interest) without penalty.

questions.question_text: Maya deposits $3,000 into a savings account that earns 4% interest compounded annually. How much will be in the account after 5 years? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: A = 3000(1.04)^5 = 3000(1.2166529) = $3,649.96. Compounding annually for 5 years at 4% grows the $3,000 deposit by about $650.

questions.question_text: Jordan invests $5,000 at 6% interest compounded semi-annually. What is the balance after 3 years? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: A = 5000(1 + 0.06/2)^(2x3) = 5000(1.03)^6 = 5000(1.194052) = $5,970.26. Semi-annual compounding means n = 2, so the rate per period is 3%.

questions.question_text: A college fund starts with $10,000 and earns 5% interest compounded monthly. How much is in the fund after 4 years? (Round to the nearest cent.)
Lisa and Mark each invest $2,000 for 10 years at 5%. Lisa's account compounds annually, while Mark's compounds quarterly. How much more does Mark earn than Lisa? (Round to the nearest cent.)
A business takes out a $20,000 loan at 8% annual interest compounded quarterly. What is the total amount owed after 2 years? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: A = 20000(1 + 0.08/4)^(4x2) = 20000(1.02)^8 = 20000(1.17165938) = $23,433.19. Quarterly compounding at 8% annual means 2% per quarter over 8 quarters.

questions.question_text: Carlos deposits $1,500 in an account earning 3.5% compounded monthly. After how many full years will his balance first exceed $1,700?

options.text: 2 years
3 years
4 years
5 years

questions.question_text: Two banks offer savings accounts. Bank A offers 4.8% compounded monthly, and Bank B offers 5% compounded annually. Which bank gives a higher balance on a $1,000 deposit after 1 year?

options.text: Bank A, because monthly compounding makes its effective rate higher than 5%
Bank B, because 5% is a higher nominal rate and the compounding difference is not enough to overcome it
They produce the exact same balance
It depends on the initial deposit amount

questions.explanation: Bank A: 1000(1 + 0.048/12)^12 = 1000(1.004)^12 = 1000(1.04907) = $1,049.07. Bank B: 1000(1.05)^1 = $1,050.00. Bank B gives $0.93 more because its 5% rate overcomes Bank A's monthly compounding advantage at 4.8%.

questions.question_text: Priya wants to have $8,000 in 6 years. If her account earns 4% compounded annually, how much should she deposit now? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: Solving for P: P = A / (1 + r)^t = 8000 / (1.04)^6 = 8000 / 1.265319 = $6,320.51. This is the present value needed to grow to $8,000 in 6 years.

questions.question_text: A grandparent invests $5,000 at 6% compounded annually when a child is born. How much will the account be worth when the child turns 18? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: A = 5000(1.06)^18 = 5000(2.854339) = $14,271.70. Over 18 years, the power of compound interest nearly triples the original $5,000 investment.

questions.question_text: A city's emergency fund of $50,000 is placed in an account earning 3% compounded quarterly. The city plans to use the fund in 5 years. How much will be available? (Round to the nearest cent.)
questions.explanation: A = 50000(1 + 0.03/4)^(4x5) = 50000(1.0075)^20 = 50000(1.161077) = $58,053.83. Quarterly compounding at 3% over 5 years results in 20 compounding periods.
